players.name: Raúl Rangel
players.bio: Young Chivas stopper earning his stripes as Mexico's future between the sticks. With 14 caps at just 26, Rangel is cool under pressure and a product of Guadalajara's famed academy. His World Cup debut could be a defining moment.
players.facts: Rangel was born on 25 Feb 2000 — making him a leap-year-adjacent baby and a Y2K goalkeeper.

players.name: Jorge Sánchez
players.bio: A tenacious right-back who has bounced across Europe's leagues with real conviction. Jorge Sánchez brings energy, grit, and 59 caps of experience to El Tri's defensive unit. PAOK's gain has been Mexico's constant.
players.facts: Sánchez has played club football in Mexico, Netherlands, and Greece — a passport busier than most diplomats.

players.name: César Montes
players.bio: A commanding centre-back with a nose for the big occasion, César Montes has been a defensive cornerstone for El Tri with 67 caps. His stint in Russia with Lokomotiv Moscow adds European steel to his repertoire.
players.facts: Montes plays club football in Moscow — one of the few Mexican internationals to have swapped Liga MX for Russian winters.

players.name: Edson Álvarez
players.bio: One of Mexico's most complete players, Edson Álvarez excels as a defensive midfielder or centre-back. With nearly 100 caps, his reading of the game and physicality make him El Tri's engine. A genuine world-class talent.
players.facts: Álvarez is so versatile he's started World Cup matches as both a CB and a DM — the ultimate footballing Swiss Army knife.

players.name: Johan Vásquez
players.bio: A composed and reliable defender, Johan Vásquez has made Serie A his home with Genoa. With 46 caps and 3 international goals for a centre-back, he's not shy in the box either. A steady hand in Mexico's backline.
players.facts: Vásquez scored at the 2022 World Cup against Poland — not bad for a defender at his first major tournament.

players.name: Érik Lira
players.bio: Érik Lira is a combative, hard-working midfielder who does the unglamorous but essential work in Cruz Azul's engine room. With 25 caps, he's establishing himself as a reliable option for El Tri's midfield battle.
players.facts: Lira was born on 8 May 2000 — the same year the PlayStation 2 launched. He's been pressing opponents ever since.

players.name: Luis Romo
players.bio: A seasoned and versatile midfielder, Luis Romo has long been a trusted figure for El Tri with 63 caps. His intelligence on the ball and ability to break up play make him a classic Mexican box-to-box midfielder.
players.facts: Romo has represented Mexico at both the Copa Oro and the World Cup — a well-stamped passport for a Liga MX stalwart.

players.name: Álvaro Fidalgo
players.bio: Born in Spain, Álvaro Fidalgo chose El Tri after shining in Liga MX with América. A technically gifted, creative midfielder now at Real Betis, he brings European flair to Mexico's setup despite just 4 caps so far.
players.facts: Fidalgo is Spanish by birth but Mexican by choice — he switched allegiance after falling in love with Liga MX and Club América.

players.name: Raúl Jiménez
players.bio: Mexico's all-time great striker, Raúl Jiménez arrives at a home World Cup with 124 caps and 45 goals. Remarkably, he returned to top-level football after a life-threatening skull fracture in 2020. A true warrior.
players.facts: Jiménez suffered a fractured skull in Nov 2020 but returned to score for Fulham — one of football's most remarkable comebacks.

players.name: Alexis Vega
players.bio: Alexis Vega is an explosive, direct winger who torments defenders with pace and trickery. A Toluca fan favourite with 52 caps, he carries El Tri's number 10 with attacking ambition on home soil in 2026.
players.facts: Vega scored a stunning solo goal at the 2023 Gold Cup, showcasing the dribbling skills that made Mexico fans fall in love with him.

players.name: Santiago Giménez
players.bio: Son of former Mexico international Christian Giménez, Santi has forged his own legacy. The AC Milan striker is lethal in front of goal and, at just 25, is the most exciting attacker in El Tri's squad right now.
players.facts: Santiago's dad Christian also played for Mexico — making them one of international football's most prolific father-son duos.

players.name: Carlos Acevedo
players.bio: A reliable and experienced shot-stopper, Carlos Acevedo has been a long-serving presence at Santos Laguna. With 7 caps, he's the dependable understudy ready to step in should Mexico's starters need a rest.
players.facts: Acevedo once saved a penalty in a Liga MX shootout that sent Santos Laguna to a title — ice in his veins at club level.

players.name: Guillermo Ochoa
players.bio: A living legend. Guillermo Ochoa returns for a record sixth World Cup at 40 years old — defying time like he defies strikers. His 2014 saves against Brazil are folklore. Memo simply refuses to be written off.
players.facts: Ochoa is the only goalkeeper in history to play at six FIFA World Cups. Six! Most players don't even manage two.

players.name: Armando González
players.bio: One of El Tri's most exciting young forwards, Armando González brings pace and directness from the Chivas academy. At just 23 with 7 caps and a goal already banked, this World Cup could be his coming-out party.
players.facts: González was born on 20 April 2003 — making him one of the youngest players at the 2026 World Cup on home turf.

players.name: Israel Reyes
players.bio: A physically imposing centre-back with Club América, Israel Reyes has accumulated 34 caps and has shown he can compete at the highest level. Young, ambitious, and determined to prove himself on the world stage.
players.facts: Reyes is one of the few defenders in El Tri's squad to have scored internationally — defenders who score never go unnoticed.

players.name: Julián Quiñones
players.bio: Colombian-born Julián Quiñones naturalised as Mexican after Liga MX stints, bringing blistering pace and direct wing play to El Tri. Now at Al-Qadsiah, his 22 caps show a player embracing his adopted football home.
players.facts: Quiñones was born in Colombia but chose to represent Mexico — proof that football loyalties can cross borders and continents.

players.name: Orbelín Pineda
players.bio: Orbelín Pineda is one of the most technically gifted midfielders Mexico has produced. With 92 caps and 12 goals, the AEK Athens man's dribbling and creativity have made him a fan favourite and a constant selection.
players.facts: Pineda's unique first name — Orbelín — is so rare even autocorrect refuses to believe it exists. He is, very much, real.

players.name: Obed Vargas
players.bio: Teenage prodigy Obed Vargas has earned one of the most eye-catching transfers in recent Mexican football history — landing at Atlético Madrid. Just 20 with 6 caps, his ceiling is stratospheric for El Tri's future.
players.facts: Vargas joined Atlético Madrid as a teenager — one of the youngest Mexicans to move to a Champions League club in recent memory.

players.name: Gilberto Mora
players.bio: At just 17, Gilberto Mora is one of the youngest players ever to feature for the Mexican national team. A composed and technically sharp midfielder at Tijuana, his World Cup inclusion is a statement of stunning intent.
players.facts: Born in 2008, Mora could become one of the youngest players ever to appear at a FIFA World Cup. Gen Z has arrived.

players.name: Mateo Chávez
players.bio: Mateo Chávez is a young left-back who has made waves in the Eredivisie with AZ. Still only 22 with 10 caps, he offers athleticism and versatility down Mexico's left flank as El Tri plan for the future.
players.facts: Chávez plays his club football in the Netherlands — following a long tradition of Mexican fullbacks thriving in the Eredivisie.

players.name: César Huerta
players.bio: César Huerta is a rapid, direct winger with a flair for the spectacular. His move to Anderlecht took his game to new levels and his 26 caps show a player growing into a key attacking option for El Tri.
players.facts: Huerta made the move to Belgian football with Anderlecht — following the well-worn path of Mexican talent heading to Europe.

players.name: Guillermo Martínez
players.bio: A clinical and experienced finisher, Guillermo Martínez brings goals and Liga MX know-how to El Tri's attack. With 12 caps and 3 international goals, he punches above his weight for a player of limited call-ups.
players.facts: Martínez averages a goal every 4 caps internationally — a strike rate that would make most full-time strikers quite jealous.

players.name: Jesús Gallardo
players.bio: A veteran left-back with 121 caps, Jesús Gallardo is one of Mexico's most experienced defenders ever. Dependable, attack-minded, and seemingly ageless, the Toluca man is a trusted cornerstone of El Tri's defence.
players.facts: Gallardo has over 120 caps — placing him among the most capped defenders in Mexican football history. A genuine Tri legend.

players.name: Luis Chávez
players.bio: A powerful defensive midfielder from Cihuatlán, Luis Chávez is renowned for his ferocious long-range shooting. His thunderbolt free-kick at the 2022 World Cup turned heads globally. Now at Dynamo Moscow, still firing.
players.facts: Chávez scored one of the fastest free-kicks ever clocked at a World Cup — his 2022 rocket against Saudi Arabia hit 112 km/h.

players.name: Roberto Alvarado
players.bio: Known as 'El Piojo', Roberto Alvarado is a tricky, energetic right winger from Salamanca, Guanajuato. A Chivas star with 67 caps, his directness and pressing make him a constant headache for opposition full-backs.
players.facts: Alvarado's nickname 'El Piojo' (The Louse) is a nod to former Mexico boss Javier Aguirre — quite the compliment in Mexican football.

players.name: Brian Gutiérrez
players.bio: Born in Berwyn, Illinois, Brian Gutiérrez is a US-born attacking midfielder who chose El Tri over the USMNT. A creative force at Guadalajara with 7 caps and 2 goals already, he's one of Mexico's most exciting young talents.
players.facts: Gutiérrez was born in Illinois and could have played for the USA — instead he chose Mexico, adding spice to the North American rivalry.
